claim: Articles published in open access format are less likely to be cited than traditional journals.

result.claim: Articles published in open access format are less likely to be cited than traditional journals.
result.reasoning: The claim that articles published in open access format are less likely to be cited than traditional journals is refuted by the evidence. Paper ID 27768226, "Open Access Increases Citation Rate," provides robust evidence to the contrary. This study, which compared citations of open-access and non-open-access articles within the same journal (Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences - PNAS), found that open-access articles gained a significant citation advantage. Specifically, OA articles were twice as likely to be cited 4 to 10 months after publication and almost three times as likely between 10 and 16 months. The study also controlled for numerous confounding factors, enhancing the credibility of its findings.

evidence.text: The Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS) announced its open-access option for authors on June 8 of the year it started.
OA articles are twice as likely to be cited 4 to 10 months after publication.
OA articles gained a significant citation advantage over non-OA articles in the 4 to 16 months following publication.
Self-archived articles are cited less often than OA articles from the same journal.
PNAS was one of the first journals to offer an open-access option to its authors.
The open-access option for authors in PNAS had an associated publication charge of US$1,000.
Springer offers an open-access option to its authors.
The author of the article is an editor of an open-access journal.
OA articles are almost three times as likely to be cited between 10 and 16 months after publication.
